While studying languages, I often thought it would be nice to try writing the Analects, but when I actually tried writing it with a brush, the handwriting looked unsightly, so I tore it up immediately and submitted it. I wondered if there was n't a useful method to learn faster and easier than the time-consuming process of "stir-frying." Eventually, I ended up writing the Analects by dipping chopsticks in ink. Since I hadn't been close for long, a body that I was somewhat satisfied with was completed, and I used all the Analects lectures and Pete's work on chopstick letters. Nothing in the world is easy. The only easy thing is going down. Chapter 7, not success or development... Ah, the road to downfall is not difficult. However, the road to rising is always difficult. It is the same for mountain climbing, studying, business, and life. It seems that such a Confucius also emphasized the necessity of the woman's spirit. Consistent with the Analects... One of the teachings of the cooperative work that appears is finding the way yourself. I hoped that if I taught one thing, you would figure out about three on your own. I used metaphorical expressions more often than direct speech. The " woman's spirit" is not needed only in school. It can be said to be a spirit of life that everyone should possess, ranging from individuals and families to organizations, corporations, and even the royal court discussing national affairs. This is especially true in the workplace. It is not about whether a job is good or bad, but rather how you perform within it that is more important. Those who do not take initiative find it difficult to achieve good results, no matter how good their job is. The same applies to the second half of life viewed from the age of 50. There is no separate distinction between a wonderful future and a shabby future; what matters more is how you use the time given to you. Those who do not take initiative find it difficult to achieve good results, no matter how many twists and turns—or rather, how many turns—of life take. When I asked how much it was, he put it in and it was more than double what my brother had. My maternal grandfather spoke freely and never got drunk even when drinking, yet he never let millet go near his lips throughout his life. After resigning from his government post and reaching the time to spend his old age, he made a small cup of liquor that could hold about ten drops, and he left home with only his lips moistened. Would you view democracy broadly? To maintain ordinariness, one realizes that ordinariness is closer to happiness than mere age. That is why people want to live without pain, sadness, injustice, oppression, displacement, or poverty. They want to live without suffering too much, without sadness, without yielding too much, without being oppressed too much, without being criticized too much, or at least without suffering a massive loss. However, maintaining ordinariness is just as difficult as upholding principles. When you reach 50, you realize that the small, ordinary things were actually great happiness. Even if it is not an expensive, fancy luxury car or a fancy amusement park, every single memory shared with children brings a feeling of happiness. A life that upholds principles and maintains ordinariness can be a more comfortable and happier life. When you enter 50, you begin to look back on your life. Through this, you keenly feel how difficult it is to live by the principles you have set for yourself. A happy life may not necessarily be a special life. The richest person is not necessarily the happiest person. If you want to become rich and live a rural life, the people living in the countryside are already happy. Ordinary people are made special. Although there was a desire to do so, special people actually dream of the small happiness of ordinary people. There is no fixed answer to life. However, a life lived by upholding principles—especially silently observing the three major principles one sets for oneself—will be beautiful and happy. Knowing one's calling, knowing understanding, and remaining silent—the final phrase of Munho contains an important standard. The principles of a purposeful life, a life lived righteously, and a life lived together are standards of life that resonate even more meaningfully at age 50. Ah, one does not need to become a philosopher to know what the purpose of life is, but it was said that he realized it precisely at the age of 50. Knowing one person is like putting the world inside, and losing one person is like losing the world. It could be a spouse, a child, a parent, a customer, a boss, a teacher, or even a mentor. [Music] Even if you live for only one day, live like a human being. The Spring and Autumn period was an era of upheaval. Each of the nine dynasties had to devise every means to survive the competition for hegemony. For the sake of victory, they did not hesitate to use any sword dance tricks or despicable methods. In the early days of the Zhou Dynasty, politics had long since disappeared. Since there was no monarch worthy of the name, high-ranking officials, on their part, [addressed] their children's interests. They looked out only for their own self-interest and did not treat the monarch as a monarch. As treason and rebellion repeated themselves, the lives of the people were a continuous series of suffering. When Confucius asked how his homeland was, the situation of the country was truly the worst, plagued by the tyranny of a new family known as the "Four Thousand." Although he did his best, he had no choice but to leave the State of Lu. He wandered the world for over 14 years, seeking to transform it into a world where benevolence and the righteous Way prevailed, but the results were utterly pathetic. [Music] Eventually, at the age of 68, he returned to Lu to teach his disciples and write the *Spring and Autumn Annals*, tasting death. He said, "If I could only hear the people say that the world is functioning rightly, I would be willing to die that very evening." It meant that if he achieved what he had so earnestly desired—to meet a world where people live like human beings, even if he lived for just one day—he would have no regrets. [Music] All living things die. It is just that the time is not fixed. No one can be an exception. Death is still an unknown world. One may know, but experiencing death is an impossible task. text: thers fear leaving due to the regret of unfulfilled desires. However, Confucius said that it is alright to die if one lifts the ball. Do not carelessly kick the briquette sieve. Have you ever been a warm person to anyone, even once? This is a single seed from the poem "I Ask You" by poet Ahn Do-hyun. Although we do not see them often these days, until not long ago, during winter, we frequently saw them on the roadside, in containers, and on hillsides—cold briquette sieves, the cold, poetry-filled briquettes. Though there are many briquette sieves filled here and there to break them into pieces, they burned their entire bodies white for their owners until dawn last night. It is that briquette group. Now at 52, I ask myself: I have not yet passed away as easily as a briquette sieve, but have I ever, even once in my life, burned my life so hotly for someone? I said I was doing it for someone, but was it truly for him? Or I reflect on whether it was for my own sake. When was the last time I truly loved myself passionately? When was the last time I truly cherished a friend? When was the last time I showed true filial piety to my parents? When was the last time I tried to look at my boss with passion? When was the last time I spent truly passionate time with the members of my community? I said I loved them, and I said I would live with all my passion, but did I not turn away directly when they were lonely and struggling?

title: 오십, 논어를 읽어야 하는 이유ㅣ오십에 읽는 논어ㅣ오디오북ㅣ1시간 20분
text: smiles of well-being. I am simply adding 1% of passion to what I do, but the world will start to change because of me. With burning passion, I will be happy, my family satisfied, and with the encouragement of those around me, things will go well. Who is to blame for me being in this state? Because of an uneducated father, because of a poor grandfather, because I couldn't have the same ancestors as others; because my hometown is in the east, so I couldn't go to school and my school bag strap broke; because I was n't born with a healthy constitution; or because I was too drunk; because my environment was too vulnerable; because I met the wrong boss; because my spouse’s heart was twisted; because my children are too selfish. It is easy to think the front side. However, Everything starts with a simple reason. It is because I failed to focus on myself. It is because I did not perceive my age with passion. It is because I was not passionate enough toward myself. When I heat myself up, everything around me that was shivering coldly begins to slowly melt. The saying, "If you hear the Way in the morning, you may die in the evening," means that if you live passionately today, you will have no regrets even if you die tomorrow.
